question: What are the conditions of eligibility to the U. S. Senate?
answer: A Senator must be thirty years of age, have been a citizen of the United States nine years, and must be an inhabitant of the State from which he is chosen.

subject: Civil Government
question: By whom and for what term are Senators chosen?
answer: Senators are chosen by the Legislatures of their respective States for a term of six years.

subject: Civil Government
question: Is the President of the Senate pro tempore, when the Vice-President has succeeded to the presidency, Vice-President of the U. S.? Give your reason.
answer: The President of the Senate pro tempore is not Vice-President of the United States. The Vice-President is an officer of the United States, and cannot be a member of Congress. A person may be eligible to the office of Senator, and consequently to the position of President pro tem., though ineligible to the VicePresidency. Example: the President pro tem. might not be a native-born citizen.

subject: Civil Government
question: When does the Chief Justice preside over the Senate?
answer: When the President is tried the Chief Justice presides.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is impeachment? By whom made? By whom tried?
answer: An impeachment is a solemn and specific accusation brought against a public officer for misconduct in office. The House has the sole power of preferring articles of impeachment. The Senate has the sole power to try all impeachments.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is an executive session of the Senate? Why so called, and what business is transacted at such session, and in what manner?
answer: An Executive Session is a special meeting of Senators called for the purpose of confirming Presidential appointments or ratifying treaties. It is so called because in such cases the Senate acts on the recommendations of the President. The meeting is held with closed doors, the members being, generally, under an injunction of secresy.

subject: Civil Government
question: (a) When is the Vice-President of the U. S. elected by the Senate? (b) When has such an election occurred?
answer: (a) When the electors shall fail to elect a Vice-President. (b) Richard M. Johnson in 1837 was elected Vice-President by the Senate.

subject: Civil Government
question: What punishment under the Constitution may follow conviction, on impeachment, by the Senate?
answer: 1st, Removal from office; and, 2nd, Disqualification to hold and enjoy any office of honor, trust, or profit under the United States.

subject: Civil Government
question: What constitutes a quorum in Congress?
answer: A majority of each House.

subject: Civil Government
question: Name the objects in taking the vote by yeas and nays in the U. S. Congress.
answer: To preserve a record of the vote of each member on any matter voted upon.

subject: Civil Government
question: On what does the duration of Congress depend?
answer: 1st. On the Constitutional limitation which cannot extend beyond two years. 2nd. On the pleasure of the two Houses, subject to the foregoing restrictions. 3rd. On the pleasure of the President of the United States, when the two Houses cannot agree on the time of adjournment.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is a bill as used in the Constitution?
answer: A bill is the draft of a proposed law.

subject: Civil Government
question: (a) By how many processes may a bill become a law? (b) Give the several steps of each process.
answer: (a) Three processes. (b) First process: 1st. The bill shall pass both Houses of Congress. 2nd. It shall be presented to the President. 3rd. If he approve it he shall sign it. Second Process: 1st. The bill shall pass both Houses. 2nd. It shall be presented to the President. 3rd. If he disapprove it, he shall return it to that House in which it originated. 4th. That House shall enter its objections at large on their journal. 5th. They shall proceed to reconsider it; and if, after such reconsideration, two-thirds of the House shall agree to pass it, it shall be sent, with the objections, to the other House. 6th. The other House shall reconsider the bill; and if approved by two-thirds of that House, it shall become a law. Third Process: 1st. The bill shall pass both Houses. 2nd. It shall be sent to the President. 3rd. He neglects to approve or return it. 4th. It becomes a law at the end of ten days (Sundays excepted), unless Congress by adjournment within that time prevent its return.

subject: Civil Government
question: (a) Is the veto power of the President qualified or absolute? (b) State what you can respecting the exercise of this power by the different Presidents.
answer: (a) The veto power of the President is only qualified, not absolute. In this respect it differs from the veto power of the British Sovereign, in whom it is absolute. (b) Nearly all the Presidents have exercised the prerogative. Tyler vetoed five bills, and Johnson, twenty-one. Bills have been passed over the veto of but three Presidents—viz., one in the administration of Mr. Tyler, four in that of Mr. Pierce, and seventeen in that of Mr. Johnson.

subject: Civil Government
question: Give an explanation of a writ of habeas corpus.
answer: It is a writ of relief ordering the release of any one imprisoned or wrongfully restrained of his liberty. If the court on inquiry decide that the person is justly restrained he is remanded to custody, otherwise he is set at liberty.

subject: Civil Government
question: When, why, and by whom may the writ of habeas corpus be suspended?
answer: The writ of habeas corpus may be suspended during a rebellion, when the public safety may require it. The power of suspension belongs to Congress. From 1861 to 1865, Congress vested the power in the President.

subject: Civil Government
question: Why are export duties from States prohibited?
answer: The Prohibition is to prevent taxing the inter-ests of any State to its detriment, and giving undue advantages to others. The production of the several States being different, the burden of taxation would be as unequal as the exports.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is the object of the provision restricting the disbursement of public money?
answer: To secure strict faithfulness in the public ex-penditures. No officer of the U. S. or of Congress can draw a dollar of public money except by appro-priations made by law.

subject: Civil Government
question: State why army appropriations cannot extend beyond two years.
answer: This constitutional provision was due to the fear that the army might become a power too formid-able to be consistent with the rights and liberties of the people.

subject: Civil Government
question: State clearly what is meant by a bill of attainder.
answer: It is a special act of the legislative body, inflicting capital punishment on a person for high crimes, without having convicted him before a court of law. A person against whom such an act was passed was said to be attainted and outlawed. His blood became so corrupted that he could neither inherit from his ancestry, nor transmit by hereditary descent to his heirs.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is an ex-post-facto law?
answer: An ex-post-facto law is one which makes an act criminal which was not so when committed.

subject: Civil Government
question: Who declares the punishment of treason? To whom is the punishment limited?
answer: Congress. The punishment is limited to the person convicted; in no legal sense does it reach his posterity.

subject: Civil Government
question: Why does the Constitution in the 14th Amendment prohibit the payment of certain debts?
answer: These debts were incurred in aid of the rebellion against the U. S. Their repudiation is designed as a penalty on those who aided the rebellion by investing in Confederate bonds.

subject: Civil Government
question: Give a comprehensive definition of civil liberty.
answer: "Civil liberty is the natural liberty of mankind so far, and only so far restrained as is consistent with the public good." — Pliny.

subject: Civil Government
question: What Constitutional provisions are made for the exercise of religious freedom?
answer: "No religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States." "Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof."

subject: Civil Government
question: What personal rights of speech, freedom of the press and right of petition are guaranteed by the Constitution?
answer: No law shall be made "abridging the freedom of speech or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for a redress of grievances."

subject: Civil Government
question: State a reason why the Senate suffrage can never be changed by Constitutional amendment.
answer: Art. V. of the Constitution provides that "No State, without its consent, shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the Senate."

subject: Civil Government
question: Why are insurrections more liable to occur under our government than under a monarchy?
answer: On account of the greater freedom our people enjoy than those under other forms of government, that freedom is, correspondingly, more liable to be abused.

subject: Civil Government
question: What rights and powers are reserved to the respective States?
answer: All such rights and powers are reserved to the States as are not expressly, or by necessary implication, delegated to the general government.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is the origin of the State obligation to the Federal Union?
answer: It is based on their assent to the Constitution of the U. S. Having accepted the terms of the union, they became subordinate to the national authority.

subject: Civil Government
question: Why are amendments equally binding on such States as do not ratify them as upon others?
answer: Because, having entered the Union, they agreed to the terms upon which its Constitution might be amended.

subject: Civil Government
question: What terms of the Constitution particularly establish the supremacy of the U. S. authority?
answer: Sect. 2, Art. VI. : ''This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof, and all treaties made, or which shall be made under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every State shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding."

subject: Civil Government
question: Name the commercial powers which the States are forbidden to exercise.
answer: 1. To coin money. 2. To issue bills of credit. 3. To make anything but gold and silver coin a tender in payment of debts. 4. To pass any law impairing the obligation of contracts

subject: Civil Government
question: Under what circumstances would a State be justified in raising troops without the consent of Congress?
answer: Necessities for protection against invasion or insurrection might be so great as to render a delay dangerous; in which case the State government would be justified in arming and equipping its own troops for the emergency.

subject: Civil Government
question: For what purpose are inspection laws enacted?
answer: Inspection laws serve to maintain the standard and purity of articles designed for exportation and domestic use.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is the term of office of Supreme Court judges?
answer: During good behavior, or life. Any judge of the U. S. Court having held his commission ten years, and having attained the age of seventy years, may resign his office and receive the same salary during life which was payable to him at the time of his resignation.

subject: Civil Government
question: What was the origin of trial by jury?
answer: Trial by jury originated in England in the 9th century during the reign of Alfred the Great.

subject: Civil Government
question: What is the difference between a grand and petit jury?
answer: A grand jury consists of from 12 to 23 men, whose duty is to make careful inquiry of offenses committed within the district for which they are chosen, and to make presentment of the same. A majority is required to find an indictment. A petit jury consists of 12 "good and lawful men," selected to try the offenses against which indictments are found, or for the trial of issues of fact. A unanimous vote in most States is necessary to convict.

subject: Civil Government
question: Under what conditions may a person be deprived of his property?
answer: The public good may require private property for public roads, railroads, arsenals, forts, etc. In these cases private property may be taken by authority of law, but not without just compensation to the owner.

subject: Civil Government
question: How is treason against the U. S. defined?
answer: "Treason against the United States shall consist in levying war against them, or adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort."

subject: Civil Government
question: In whom is the executive power of the U. S. vested?
answer: In the President of the United States.

subject: Civil Government
question: What conditions of eligibility are required in the President?
answer: He must be a natural born citizen of the U. S., must have attained the age of 35 years, and have been 14 years a resident within the United States.

subject: Civil Government
question: How many and what methods are provided for the election of President of the U. S.?
answer: Two methods: first, by electors appointed for that purpose; if this method fail the election devolves on the House of Representatives.

subject: Civil Government
question: Give a brief outline of the method of electing the President of the United States.
answer: The following are the steps taken in the election of President: 1st. The electors, previously appointed, shall meet in their respective States on the first Wednesday in December, and vote by distinct ballots for President and Vice-President. 2nd. The votes are signed, certified, sealed, and addressed to the President of the Senate at the seat of government of the United States. 3rd. The President of the Senate, at a joint meeting of the two Houses of Congress, called the second Wednesday of February, shall open the certificates, and the votes are counted by tellers appointed by the House and the Senate. 4th. The person having the greatest number of votes for President, shall be President, if such number be a majority of the whole number of the electors appointed. 5th. If no person have a majority of the electors appointed, the election of President devolves on the House of Representatives.

subject: Civil Government
question: Who would become President if the Constitutional methods prescribed failed in the election of that officer?
answer: The Vice-President.

subject: Civil Government
question: State the extent of power given the President over pardons and reprieves.
answer: " He shall have power to grant reprieves and pardons for offenses against the United States, except in the cases of impeachment."

subject: Botany
question: State resemblances and differences between plants and animals.
answer: Plants, like animals, are organized bodies composed of distinct parts, endowed with vitality but without sensation. Like animals, the presence of every organ is essential to completeness in plants, but the power of volition and perception of animals is absent in plants.

subject: Botany
question: What is meant by the flora of a country?
answer: The flora of a country is the system of vegetable species native in a given region or locality.

subject: Botany
question: Explain the application of the terms species and genus.
answer: The term species embraces such individual plants as may have originated from a common stock, bearing essential resemblances to each other as well as to their common parent. A genus is an assemblage of species closely related to each other in the structure of their flowers and fruit.

subject: Botany
question: (a) How are plants designated as to their term of life? (b) Define and give examples of the terms you employ.
answer: (a) Annual, biennial, and perennial. (b) An annual plant continues but one season; as, oats, corn; a biennial germinates and bears leaves only the first season, and blossoms, bears fruit and dies the second; as, cabbage, turnip, parsnip; a perennial continues many years; as, trees and shrubs.

subject: Botany
question: Distinguish between deciduous and evergreen trees, and give two illustrations of each.
answer: Deciduous trees lose their foliage in autumn; as, oak, hickory; evergreens retain their leaves and verdure throughout the season; as, pines, magnolias.

subject: Botany
question: (a) Define germination. (b) What does it re-quire?
answer: (a) Germination is the awakening and development of the embryonic plant within the seed. (b) It requires warmth, moisture, air, and shade.

subject: Botany
question: Name and define the parts of the embryo.
answer: The embryo consists of the radicle, the descending part, forming the root; the plumule, a stem bud, which forms the ascending axis of the plant; and the cotyledons, the seed lobes, destined to become the first leaves of the plant, shown in the two thick leaves of the bean as it emerges from the ground.

subject: Botany
question: Upon what characteristic of the early growing plant is founded the most important subdivision of flowering plants?
answer: The number of cotyledons; as, monocotyledons and dicotyledons.

subject: Botany
question: Define and apply correctly the terms endogens and exogens.
answer: Endogens are plants which grow by internal accretions; as, wheat, grass; exogens are those whose stems grow by external accretions; as forest trees.

subject: Botany
question: What distinction is observed between the leaves of endogenous and exogenous plants?
answer: Endogenous plants have, in most cases, parallel veined leaves, while leaves of exogenous plants are net veined.

subject: Botany
question: Give a diagram-outline of the vegetable sub-kingdoms known as Phaenogamia and Cryptogamia.
answer: Cohorts. Examples. Phænogamia. PROVINCE I. EXOGENS, or DICOTYLEDONS. CLASS I. ANGIOSPERMS. CLASS II. GYMNOSPERMS. A. Dialypetalæ, or Polypetalous Exogens—Roseworts. B. Gamopetalæ, or Monopetalous Exogens—Phloxworts. C. Apetalæ, or Apetalous Exogens—Pokeworts. D. Conoideæ, or Cone-bearing Plants—Pines. PROVINCE II. ENDOGENS, or MONOCOTYLEDONS. CLASS III. PETALIFERÆ, or AGLUMACEÆ. CLASS IV. GLUMIFERÆ. E. Spadicifloræ—Aroids. F. Florideæ—Lilyworts. G. Graminoideæ—Grasses. Cryptogamia. PROVINCE III. ACROGENS, The Point-Growers. CLASS V. ANGIOSPORES. H. Lycopodales—Lycopods. I. Filicales—Ferns. J. Muscales—Mosses. PROVINCE IV. THALLOGENS, The Mass-Growers. CLASS VI. GYMNOSPORES. K. Lichenales—Lichens. L. Algales—Seaweeds. M. Fungales—Fungi.

subject: Botany
question: Name and locate the parts of a flower.
answer: The floral envelopes and the essential floral organs. The floral envelopes consist of one or more circles of leaves surrounding the essential organs. The outer circle is called the calyx; the inner, if any be present, is called the corolla. Within the envelopes are the stamens, small thread-like organs varying in number from one to one hundred. The pistils occupy the center of the flower.

subject: Botany
question: Define the terms, perianth, androecium, gynoecium.
answer: Perianth is used to designate both the calyx and corolla; the stamens are collectively called androecium; gynoecium is used for the entire collection of pistils.

subject: Botany
question: Enumerate the attributes possessed by a typical flower.
answer: The typical flower should be complete, regular, symmetrical, alternating, and each organ distinct.

subject: Botany
question: Explain the terms used in distinguishing the typical flower.
answer: The term complete implies that the four sets of organs are arranged in as many concentric circles; regular, that the organs of the same name are all similar; symmetrical, that it has the same number of organs in each circle; alternating, that the several organs in each set stand not opposite to, but alternating with the organs of the adjacent set; distinct, that all organs are free from each other.

subject: Botany
question: How are leaves of the calyx and corolla designated ?
answer: The leaves of the calyx are styled sepals, those of the corolla, petals.

subject: Botany
question: When is a flower said to be (a) perfect? (b) complete? (c) apetalous? (d) staminate? (e) pistillate?
answer: (a) A flower is perfect when it has both stamens and pistils. (b) It is complete when it has stamens, pistils, calyx, and corolla. (c) It is apetalous when the calyx is present without the corolla. (d) It is staminate when it has stamens without pistils. (e) It is pistillate when it has pistils without stamens.

subject: Botany
question: What are glumes and pales?
answer: Glumes and pales represent the floral envelopes of the grasses.

subject: Botany
question: What is pollen? Where found?
answer: Pollen is the fecundating yellow dust contained in the cells of anthers.

subject: Botany
question: Define the term pericarp, and state which form is dehiscent and which indehiscent.
answer: The pericarp is the envelope inclosing the seed. The fleshy pericarp, as the berry, apple, cherry, etc., is indehiscent ; the dry pericarp, as the pea, mustard, etc., is dehiscent.

subject: Botany
question: Give examples of the following: Drupe, Tryma, Pome, Hesperidium, Samara.
answer: Drupe, — cherry, peach; Tryma, — butternut, hickorynut; Pome, — apple, haw; Hesperidium, — orange, lemon; Samara, — ash, maple.

subject: Botany
question: What are the offices of the root?
answer: 1st. To support the plant in position. 2nd. To imbibe from the soil the food necessary to the growth of the plant.

subject: Botany
question: Name some of the principal forms of roots.
answer: The principal axial forms are the ramous, fusiform, napiform, and conical; the principal inaxial forms are fibrous, tubercular, coralline, nodulous, and moniliform.

subject: Botany
question: State the difference between epiphytes and parasites.
answer: Epiphytes, sometimes called air-plants, are those whose roots are fixed upon other plants, while the epiphyte itself derives its nourishment wholly from the air. Parasites are those whose roots, penetrating to the cambium layer of other plants and trees, appropriate the stolen juices to their own growth.

subject: Botany
question: What terms are applied to those branches tending to produce adventitious roots?
answer: Cions, suckers, stolons, offsets, slips, layers, cuttings, and runners.

subject: Botany
question: Define the terms caulis, culm, trunk.
answer: Caulis is the term applied to the annual leaf-stems of herbaceous plants. The culm is the stem of the grasses and the sedges. The term trunk is applied to the stems of trees.

subject: Botany
question: What peculiarity is observed with respect to the twining of different vines?
answer: The hop vine invariably winds with the sun, that is, from left to right; while others, as the morn-ing-glory, revolves in a contrary direction.

subject: Botany
question: What distinction can you name between the rhizome and the creeper?
answer: The rhizome is a prostrate, fleshy, rooting stem, often marked with scars, as in bloodroot; while the creeper is more slender, much branched, many jointed, and sends out rootlets in every direction, binding the soil into turfs wherever it abounds.

subject: Botany
question: Is the tuber a root? State your reason.
answer: The tuber is not a root. It is the thickened portion of the subterranean stem and produces buds.

subject: Botany
question: What is a bulb? Name its most common forms.
answer: The bulb is a thickened mass of scales with a small axis, the whole forming a bud. The most com-mon forms are tunicated, as the onion, and scaly, as in the lily.

subject: Botany
question: How does a flower-bud differ from a leaf-bud?
answer: A leaf-bud contains the rudiments of a leafy stem or branch folded up in such a manner as to occupy as little space as possible; the flower-bud con-tains the same elements transformed into the rudimen-tary organs of a flower.

subject: Botany
question: What various positions upon the plant may the leaf-bud occupy?
answer: With regard to position, the leaf-bud may be terminal or axillary. In addition to these, buds may be accessory or adventitious.

subject: Botany
question: Define vernation, and enumerate the various forms of bud folding.
answer: Vernation signifies the mode of arrangement and folding of the leaf organs within the bud. The following terms denote the leaf folding within the bud : reclined, conduplicate, plicate, circinate, convolute, involute, revolute.

subject: Botany
question: What are the common forms of leaf arrangement on the axis?
answer: Alternate; i.e., one above another on opposite sides. Scattered; i.e., irregularly spiral. Opposite; i.e., two against each other, at the same node. Resolute; i.e., clustered regularly.

subject: Botany
question: Name the parts of a complete net-veined leaf.
answer: Blade, petiole, stipules, margin, apex, base, midvein, veins, veinlets, venules.

subject: Botany
question: How do leaves contribute to the nourishment of plants?
answer: The leaves are the organs of respiration and digestion, without which the plant would soon die.

subject: Botany
question: What is the characteristic venation of the three grand divisions of the vegetable kingdom?
answer: The leaves of Exogens, or Dicotyledons, are net-veined; those of Endogens, or Monocotyledons, are parallel-veined; the leaves of Cryptogams are fork-veined, dividing and subdividing in a forked manner.

subject: Botany
question: Name and define ten of the most common forms of leaf-outlines.
answer: Ovate, having the outline of an egg; orbicular, circular; lanceolate, lance-shaped, tapering gradually toward the apex; deltoid, like the Greek letter Δ; elliptical, formed like an ellipse; cordate, heart-shaped; auriculate, ear shaped lobes at base; sagittate, arrow-shaped; cuneate, wedge-shaped; reniform, kidney-shaped.

subject: Botany
question: What are the divisions of a compound leaf called? To what are they usually attached?
answer: The divisions of a compound leaf are called leaflets, and are usually attached to the rachis.

subject: Botany
question: Give the terms used to describe the margin of leaves.
answer: Dentate, toothed; serrate, having teeth pointing forward; crenate, with rounded teeth; undulate, wavy-edged; repand, indented like the margin of an umbrella; spinous, projecting veins; incised, cut; crispate, crisped.

subject: Botany
question: What terms are employed in describing the surface of leaves?
answer: The leaf surface may be glabrous (smooth), scabrous (rough), pubescent, covered with soft, short hair, villous, when the hairs are long and weak, sericeous, when the hair is fine and silky, lanuginous, wooly, tomentous, matted like felt, floccose, when soft and fleecy.

subject: Botany
question: What is a tendril and its use?
answer: A tendril is a thread-like appendage furnished to weak-stemmed plants as their means of support.

subject: Botany
question: What do you mean by the term metamorphosis, as applied to flowers?
answer: The metamorphosis of a flower is the transfor- mation of the leaf to form the flower.

subject: Botany
question: (a) Define inflorescence. (b) Name the two forms.
answer: (a) Inflorescence is the arrangement of flow- ers on the stem. (b) The two forms are axillary, in which the flowers originate from axillary buds, and terminal, in which the flower buds are terminal.

subject: Botany
question: What is the peduncle? Pedicel?
answer: The peduncle is the flower stalk. Divisions of the peduncle are termed pedicels.

subject: Botany
question: Name the principal varieties of axillary inflorescence.
answer: The spike, spadix, catkin, raceme, corymb, umbel, panicle, thyrse, and head are the principal varieties of axillary inflorescence.

subject: Botany
question: Describe the following: umbel, spadix, panicle, catkin, raceme.
answer: An umbel consists of several pedicels of nearly the same length radiating from the same point; a spadix is a thick, fleshy rachis, with flowers closely sessile or imbedded on it; a panicle is a compound in- florescence formed by an irregular branching of the pedicels of the raceme, as in oats; the catkin is a slender, pendent spike with scaly bracts, as in the oak and willow; the raceme is a rachis bearing its flowers on distinct, simple pedicels.

subject: Botany
question: Name the parts of the anther.
answer: Filament, anther-lobe, connective, and valves.

subject: Botany
question: Define and give an example of multiple fruits.
answer: Multiple fruits are formed by the union of many separate flowers, as the pineapple.

subject: Botany
question: How are plants nourished, clothed, protected?
answer: The first nourishment the plant requires is de- rived from the albumen contained within the seed; afterward the sap, laden with the requisite food elements, permeates every tissue, and deposits within each organ its appropriate food. The leaves and other parts of plants are covered with hairs, which, with the bark, serve as clothing. The sharp thorns, spikes and prickles, with which many plants are supplied, would seem to imply a provision for self-defense.

subject: Botany
question: (a) What are cryptogams? (b) Give illustrations. (c) Where do they grow? (d) What useful purpose do they serve?
answer: (a) Cryptogams are flowerless plants. (b) Mosses, ferns, lichens, seaweed, mushrooms. (c) They grow on rocks, sand, tree-trunks, cinders, etc. (d) They form the basis of all vegetable and animal life. By the decay of successive generations of these simple forms soil is formed and fertilized, and the growth of higher orders, as grains, grasses and trees, is rendered possible.

subject: Botany
question: What is the elementary organism of the plant?
answer: The cell, a closed sac of membrane containing a fluid, by its multiplied aggregation, makes up the mass of all vegetation.

subject: Botany
question: Name the different forms of cells.
answer: Spiral, annular, porous, and reticulated.

subject: Botany
question: What chemical elements compose the outer and secondary cell walls?
answer: Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.

subject: Botany
question: Name the contents of the vegetable cell.
answer: While some cells contain nothing but air and others solid matter, the greater number are filled with both fluids and solids. These are the cytoblast, a globular atom, designed to form new cells, and protoplasm, the nourishing semi-fluid.

subject: Botany
question: What is chlorophyll?
answer: Chlorophyl is the green coloring matter of leaves.

subject: Botany
question: (a) Of what does the growth of plants consist? (b) Explain the process by which this is accomplished.
answer: (a) The growth of plants consists of the development of new cells. (b) The primordial utricle divides into two or more parts by new walls growing from its sides till they meet, and thus cells multiplying by millions, build up the fabric of the plant.

subject: Botany
question: What different forms of tissue are formed of cells?
answer: Cellular tissue, termed parenchyma; fibrous tissue, or pleurenchyma; vascular tissue, or trachenchyma; laticiferous tissue, or cienchyma.

subject: Botany
question: Describe the tissues found in vegetation.
answer: Parenchyma, the most common form of tissue, is composed of spheroidal cells. It is found in all young growths,—the pith, leaf, and stem; in the pulp of fruits, and in the soft parts of all plants. Pleurenchyma consists of elongated cells cohering by their sides in such a way as to form continuous fibre, as in flax and hemp. Trachenchyma is a tissue of vessels and tubes. These extend lengthwise and form rows of cells joined end to end, and fuse into one by the absorption of the contiguous walls. Cienchyma is a system of milk vessels, secreting the peculiar juice of the plant, as opium, gamboge, resin, etc.

subject: Botany
question: (a) What are the breathing-pores of plants? (b) How are these affected by moist and dry weather?
answer: (a) Little chinks in the leaf-epidermis, termed stomata. (b) Each stoma is guarded by cells of such construction as to open in moist weather and close in dry.

subject: Botany
question: (a) What is the construction of vegetable glands? (b) What is their use?
answer: (a) Glands are cellular structures within the epidermis, or at the base of a hair, or at its summit. (b) Their use is to elaborate and contain the peculiar secretions of plants, such as oils, resins, honey, poisons, etc.

subject: Botany
question: Into what four classes is the vegetable kingdom, according to modes of growth, divided?
answer: Into Exogens, or outside-growers, Endogens, or inside-growers, Acrogens, or point-growers, and Thallogens, or mass-growers.

subject: Botany
question: Name and locate the structural parts of Exogens.
answer: 1st. The pith, consisting of parenchyma, which occupies the central part of the stem. 2nd. The medullary sheath, a thin, delicate tissue, composed of spiral vessels, which immediately surrounds the pith. 3rd. The wood proper, which is arranged in concentric zones or layers about the central mass. This consists of two kinds — the sap-wood and heart-wood. 4th. The bark, covering and protecting the wood. It consists of the three parts — the inner or white bark, the middle or green bark, and the outer or brown bark.

subject: Botany
question: What and where is the cambium layer?
answer: The cambium layer is a mucilaginous-sap solution of the starchy deposits of the preceding year. It is between the wood and white bark, serving to loosen the latter, and thus render it easy to peel from the wood.

subject: Botany
question: State the characteristic structure of endogenous plants.
answer: The stem of an endogenous plant is composed of tissues similar to those of the exogenous stem, but there is no distinction of bark, wood, pith, or annual layers in the endogen.

subject: Botany
question: Upon what peculiarity of growth is based the distinction of Exogens and Endogens ?
answer: Upon the characteristic method of annual accretions; — in the Exogens the yearly increments are added to the outside, while the addition of new material in the Endogens is to the interior.

subject: Botany
question: Describe the mode of growth in Acrogens.
answer: The stems of Acrogens advance beneath or above the ground, full formed, growing only at the end.

subject: Botany
question: Name some of the lowest forms of vegetation, and state to what class they belong.
answer: Mildew, frog spittle, lichens, seaweeds, puff-balls and mushrooms, belonging to the Thallogens, or mass-growers, are among the lowest forms of the vegetable kingdom.

subject: Botany
question: Show wherein animal life is dependent upon the vegetable kingdom.
answer: The process of vegetation consists in imbibing the crude matters of the earth and air, and elaborating into food the elements which animals require for their growth and sustenance.

subject: Botany
question: Of what does the substance of plants mainly consist, and how and whence is this derived?
answer: The substance of plants consists mainly of water derived from the moisture of the air and the soil, through the absorptive powers of the leaves and roots.

subject: Botany
question: Name the vital phenomena upon which the life of a plant depends ?
answer: Absorption, circulation, exhalation, assimilation, and secretion.

subject: Botany
question: In what respect are some plants and insects mutually dependent ?
answer: The propagation and continuance of certain species of plants in a given locality require cross-fertilization. This is effected sometimes by insects, which, attracted by the brilliant tints and savory juices contained within the nectary, in return for the honey they extract, carry the fertilizing pollen from the anther to distant plants of the same species.

subject: Botany
question: How are generic and specific terms in Botany distinguished?
answer: Generic names are nouns, and should always begin with capitals. Specific names are generally adjectives, and should not begin with capitals except when derived from the name of a country or person, or when the term may be a noun.

subject: Botany
question: Upon what principle is the absorption of fluids in a direction contrary to gravitation?
answer: It is due to the physical principles of capillary attraction and endosmose.

subject: Botany
question: What fact proves the importance of a rotation of crops in agriculture ?
answer: If wheat, buckwheat, peas, and cabbage be grown upon the same land, it will be observed that the wheat will select the silica, the buckwheat, the magnesia, the pea, the lime, and the cabbage the potash, each for its peculiar want.

subject: Botany
question: Describe the upward and downward flow of the sap and its use in the plant economy.
answer: Starting from the roots as crude sap, in the form of colorless water, charged with minute quantities of gases and mineral salts, it passes upward dissolving the dextrine and sugar of the cells and gaining in density, till it reaches the leaves, where it parts with a large portion of the water by exhalation, and receives carbon in return. After undergoing important chemical changes, under the action of the air and light, it becomes rich in nutritive material and returns upon its downward course through the barky tissues, distributing to every organ its due proportion of appropriate food.

subject: Botany
question: In what respect are plants and animals mutually dependent ?
answer: The carbonic acid exhaled by animals if left to accumulate would in time destroy all animal life upon the globe. The necessities of the plant demand this gas, and in the very process of its appropriation the life-giving oxygen, so essential to animal existence, is returned by the plant to the atmosphere.

subject: Physiology
question: Define the terms Anatomy, Physiology, and Hygiene.
answer: Anatomy is the description of the form and position of the organs of animal bodies. Physiology is the description of the uses of these organs. Hygiene treats of the preservation of health.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the primary animal tissues.
answer: The primary tissues are the fibrous, areolar, cartilaginous, which collectively form the connective tissues, and the adipose, sclerous, muscular, tubular, and nervous tissues.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) What are membranes? (b) State their uses.
answer: (a) Membranes are thin, extended, soft, transparent tissues, formed by fibers interwoven like network. (b) They serve to cover some part of the body, or to absorb or secrete certain fluids.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the principal membranes of the body.
answer: The principal membranes are the basement, serous, synovial, and mucous membranes.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the number and names of the ultimate elements composing the human body?
answer: There are fourteen ultimate elements, viz.: oxygen, hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, calcium, potassium, sodium, chlorine, phosphorus, sulphur, silicon, iron, magnesium, and fluorine.

subject: Physiology
question: Describe the bones and state their uses.
answer: The bones are firm and hard, combining strength and lightness. They serve as a framework or skeleton for preserving the shape of the body and for the protection of its most delicate organs, and for the attachment of muscles.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the composition of bone at different periods of life?
answer: The bones consist of animal and mineral matter. In early life the animal matter predominates; at maturity the proportion is about one part of animal to two parts of mineral matter; while, as age advances, the quantity of mineral matter greatly exceeds that of animal substance.

subject: Physiology
question: How many bones in the human body?
answer: The number of bones varies at different periods of life. In childhood the number is greater than in later life, many bones consisting of two parts growing together, forming but one bone in the adult. The number usually given, exclusive of the teeth, is 208. 9. The Head. The Cranium. Frontal bone. Two Parietal bones. Two Temporal bones. Sphenoid bone. Ethmoid bone. Occipital bone. The Face. Two Superior Maxillary bones. Inferior Maxillary bone. Two Malar bones. Two Lachrymal bones. Two Turbinated bones. Two Nasal bones. Vomer. Two Palate bones. The Ears. Hammer. Anvil. Stirrup. The Trunk. The Spinal Column. Cervical Vertebræ. Dorsal Vertebræ. Lumbar Vertebræ. The Ribs. True Ribs. False Ribs. The Sternum. The Os Hyoides. The Pelvis. Two Innominata. Sacrum. Coccyx. The Limbs. The Upper Limbs. The Shoulder. Clavicle (2). Scapula (2). The Arm. Humerus. Radius and Ulna. The Hand.

subject: Physiology
question: Draw an outline showing the relative position of the different bones in the human skeleton.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) Name and illustrate the different kinds of movable joints. (b) State how their movements are facilitated.
answer: (a) Movable joints are of three kinds — the Planiform, the Hinge, and the Ball and Socket joints. The Planiform joint is found where gliding movements are required, as in the carpus and metacarpus; the hinge joint, where there is motion in two directions only, backward and forward, as in the knee and the elbow; the ball and socket joint, where there is free movement in all directions, as in the hip and shoulder joints. (b) They are provided with cartilage where the surfaces are in contact, and supplied with synovial membrane and connecting ligaments.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) With what are the bones covered? (b) What is the purpose of this covering? (c) What peculiar disease originates beneath this covering?
answer: (a) The bones are covered, as with a sac, by a dense, white, fibrous membrane called periosteum. (b) It serves to transmit blood-vessels into the bone, thus furnishing nutriment, and gives insertion to muscles, tendons, and ligaments. (c) The disease called “felon” commences in or beneath the periosteum.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the use of the synovia?
answer: The use of the synovia is that of a lubricating fluid, enabling the surface of the bones to move more freely upon each other, preventing friction and consequent wear.

subject: Physiology
question: What mechanical and botanical principle is illustrated in the long bones?
answer: The shafts of long bones are made hollow, giving not only lightness but strength, according to the principle of mechanics, that with a given amount of material, a hollow cylinder will sustain more weight than a solid one of the same dimensions. The culms of grasses illustrate the same principle.

subject: Physiology
question: State the process by which nature repairs a broken bone.
answer: The blood which flows in consequence of the injury is gradually absorbed, and gives place to a watery fluid, which, thickening from day to day, acquires at the end of two weeks, the consistency of jelly. This begins to harden by a deposit of new bone-substance, until in five or six weeks, the broken bone is united.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) What are sprains? (b) What care do they require?
answer: (a) Sprains are caused by a twisting, straining, or tearing of the connecting ligaments of bones from their attachments. (b) As a sprain may be as serious as a broken bone, care should be exercised, lest the use of the limb before the ligament is restored to its place may impair its usefulness.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) Name the characteristic property and law of muscles. (b) What diversity of form is observed in the adaptation of the muscles to their various position and uses?
answer: (a) The characteristic property of muscles is contractility, and the law is that they shall contract toward the center. (b) The general forms or shapes of muscles are spindle-shaped, radiate, penniform, bi-penniform, and orbicular.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the general arrangement of muscles with respect to each other?
answer: With the exception of twelve single muscles, they are all arranged in pairs, each having its antagonist; so that as they contract and expand alternately, the bone to which they are attached is moved to and fro.

subject: Physiology
question: Into what two classes, with respect to action, are the muscles divided? Illustrate each class.
answer: Into voluntary, those which are under the control of the will, as the muscles of the limbs, and involuntary, those which cannot be controlled by the will, as the muscles of the heart.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) How many muscles in the human body? (b) How are the muscles of the limbs distinguished as to their use?
answer: (a) The number exceeds five hundred. (b) In regard to their use the limb muscles may be distinguished as flexors, those used to bend the limbs, and extensors, those which are used to extend the limbs.

subject: Physiology
question: Name two important muscles in each of the following regions: head, front part of trunk, back part of trunk, upper limbs, lower limbs.
answer: Head and neck: oral, orbicular and masseter; front part of trunk: the external oblique and the straight abdominal; back part of trunk: the trapezius and the superior serrated muscles; the upper limbs: the biceps and the radio-carpal extensor; the lower limbs: the gluteal and sartorius.

subject: Physiology
question: Name some of the uses which muscles serve in the animal economy.
answer: They give form and symmetry to the body, enclose the cavities, and form a firm, defensive, but yielding wall in the trunk, cover and move the limbs, and give to some of the joints their principal protection. By means of muscular action, the heart beats, the blood circulates, and respiration is carried on.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) Of what are tendons composed? (b) State their use.
answer: (a) Tendons are composed of the inelastic, white-fibrous tissue, and possess great strength. (b) They serve to convey the contractile power of muscles to the bones, and connect the muscles with the bones.

subject: Physiology
question: Show how the bones are used as levers of the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd classes.
answer: The movements of the head illustrate a lever of the first class: the back or front is the weight, the backbone is the fulcrum, and the muscles at the back or front of the neck are the powers by which we toss or bow the head. Raising the body on tiptoe illustrates a lever of the second class: the toes resting on the ground is the fulcrum, the weight of the body is the weight, and the muscles of the calf, the power. Raising the lower jaw illustrates a lever of the third class. Another familiar example is the elbow, in which the fulcrum is at the joint, the weight is the forearm and hand, and the power is in the biceps and brachial muscles.

subject: Physiology
question: State the effect upon the muscles of (a) use (b) disuse, (c) misuse.
answer: (a) By judicious use muscle grows larger, and becomes hard, compact, and darker-colored. (b) By disuse, it decreases in size, and becomes soft, flabby and pale. (c) By misuse, the muscle is often strained and blood vessels burst in our efforts to perform feats beyond our muscular power.

subject: Physiology
question: Why should one abstain from severe exercise immediately before and after eating?
answer: Because the vigor of the system is needed for the digestive functions. Nature can sustain in vigorous activity but one function at a time.

subject: Physiology
question: State the proper method and necessary extent of educating muscles.
answer: The training of muscles to the performance of any exercise in which the highest excellence is desired should be begun in early life, and continued regularly and systematically till every muscle and every fiber is under the control of the will.

subject: Physiology
question: How does the character of a child's early muscular training determine his habits of action in later life?
answer: An individual's speech, writing, singing, attitudes, walking, and actions are all determined by his first movements in these exercises. If exactness is not required in the beginning, and continued during the formative period of habit, awkwardness and impropriety must always characterize the exercises and movements of the individual through life.

subject: Physiology
question: What organs constitute the nutritive apparatus?
answer: The nutritive apparatus includes the digestive, the absorptive, the circulatory, the assimilatory, and the respiratory organs.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the digestive organs.
answer: The digestive organs are the mouth, teeth, salivary glands, palate, pharynx, œsophagus, stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and spleen.

subject: Physiology
question: What preparatory changes does food undergo before it becomes nourishment for the body?
answer: 1st. Digestion, by which the food is reduced to a soluble condition. 2nd. Absorption, by which, when digested, it is imbibed into the blood. 3rd. Circulation, which carries the enriched blood to the various parts of the system. 4th. Assimilation, by which each tissue derives from the blood the materials necessary for its support.

subject: Physiology
question: Name, locate, and state the use of the salivary glands.
answer: The salivary glands consist of three glands on each side of the mouth. The parotid gland is situated in front of the external ear and behind the angle of the lower jaw. The submaxillary gland is situated within the angle of the lower jaw. The sublingual gland is situated on the floor of the mouth, beneath the side of the tongue. They all secrete a liquid called saliva.

subject: Physiology
question: Name and locate the fluids involved in the process of digestion.
answer: 1st. The saliva in the mouth; 2nd. The gastric juice, in the stomach; 3rd. The bile, in the small intestines. 4th. The pancreatic juice, in the small intestines. 5th. The intestinal juice, in the small intestines.

subject: Physiology
question: Trace a particle of food from the mouth until it reaches the blood.
answer: From the mouth, where the food is chewed and insalivated, it passes through the pharynx and œsophagus at the cardiac orifice into the stomach. Here it undergoes a churning process until every particle is subjected to the action of the gastric juice, which digests the albuminoid substances in it, and changes them to albuminose. The work of the stomach being completed, the food passes through the pyloric orifice into the small intestines, where it is subjected to the pancreatic fluid, the bile and the intestinal juice, which digest the starch and fat, changing the starch into sugar and the fat into chyle. Here the digested mass, called collectively the chyle, is absorbed by the lacteals and blood vessels and taken into the general circulation.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the principal use of the saliva?
answer: It moistens the food and thus facilitates digestion.

subject: Physiology
question: State the conditions upon which the health of the digestive organs depend.
answer: 1st. The quantity of food; this should simply equal the waste of the system. 2nd. The quality of food, which should be nutritive and digestible. 3rd. The manner of taking food. Food should be neither too hot nor too cold; it should be sufficiently masticated and taken at regular intervals. 4th. The system should be mentally and physically conditioned to receive food.

subject: Physiology
question: Under what circumstances is digestion most perfect?
answer: When the action of the cutaneous vessels is energetic, the mind free from absorbing thought, excitement or depression, the blood well purified, and the muscular system duly exercised.

subject: Physiology
question: What does the term absorption comprehend?
answer: Absorption is the general term for that process by which all soluble substances, external to the animal body, are introduced into the tissues of the body. It includes also the process by which portions of the living tissues are themselves removed, or absorbed within the body.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the absorbents.
answer: The absorbents are the lymphatic vessels of the small intestines, termed lacteals, lymphatic glands, vessels, and ducts, and the thoracic duct.

subject: Physiology
question: What are the principal secretions and excretions of the human body?
answer: Mucus, sebaceous matter, perspiration, tears, serous fluid, saliva, gastric juice, pancreatic juice, intestinal juice, and bile.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the length of time required for digestion?
answer: From two to four hours, according to the nature of food, state of the system and perfection of mastication.

subject: Physiology
question: State the object of cooking food, and how this is secured.
answer: The object of cooking food is to render it more palatable and easily digested. Cooking breaks the cells and softens the fibers of which the food is composed.

subject: Physiology
question: Mention the evils arising from rapid eating.
answer: 1st. The food is swallowed without sufficient saliva. 2nd. The particles of food are so large as to hinder the action of the gastric juice, which is often weakened by the use of drinks poured down with the food. 3rd. We do not realize the quantity eaten until the stomach is overloaded. 4th. Failing to get the taste of our food we think it insipid, and resort to condiments which over stimulate the digestive organs.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the organs of circulation.
answer: The heart, arteries, capillaries, and veins.

subject: Physiology
question: Describe the heart.
answer: The heart is the hollow muscle enclosed in a sac, termed pericardium. It is situated between the lungs. It is conical in shape, the apex pointing downward, forward, and to the left. Its only attachments are the large blood-vessels by which it is joined to the vertebral column.

subject: Physiology
question: Why is there sometimes said to be two hearts?
answer: On account of the division of the heart by a muscular septum into two parts — the right and left. Each of these contains two apartments, termed auricles and ventricles, the ventricles being next the apex. The two sides have no communication, each performing a separate function. The right is sometimes termed the pulmonic heart, the left, the systemic heart.

subject: Physiology
question: Describe the circulation of blood, distinguishing between pulmonary and systemic circulation.
answer: In pulmonic circulation the right auricle after receiving the blood from two large veins, contracts and sends it into the right ventricle. This in turn contracts and sends it through the pulmonary artery to the lungs, where it is purified and becomes a bright arterial blood. Here the systemic circulation begins by the entrance of the blood into the left auricle, which, contracting, sends it to the left ventricle, from which it is transmitted through the aorta to all parts of the system, from which it returns through the capillaries and veins to the right auricle to repeat its course as before.

subject: Physiology
question: How does the blood differ in color in different parts of its circulation? State the cause.
answer: As the blood visits the different organs it both gives out and gathers up materials. In the arterial circulation it is enriched with food, and scarlet with oxygen received in the lungs; returning from the organs it has fed, it is impoverished in quality and purple in color.

subject: Physiology
question: What time is required for the complete circulation of the blood through the system?
answer: The entire mass is believed to make the circuit in from one to two minutes.

subject: Physiology
question: How is a backward flow of blood prevented?
answer: A backward flow in the auricles is prevented by the contraction of muscular fibers about the mouths of the veins, and by valves in the veins.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the normal temperature of the body?
answer: About 98° Fahrenheit.

subject: Physiology
question: Which end of a ruptured artery should be tied? Why?
answer: The end next to the heart, because the blood in arteries flows from the heart.

subject: Physiology
question: Define coagulation, and state its use.
answer: Coagulation is a thickening or hardening of the blood. It is serviceable in stopping the flow of blood from a wounded vein.

subject: Physiology
question: State the use of circulation of the blood.
answer: Circulation of the blood carries nutrition to all parts of the system, and also carries impurities to the lungs to be exhaled.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the three classes of organic substances used for food.
answer: Farinaceous, saccharine, and albuminous.

subject: Physiology
question: How do you account for the arteries being deep-seated, while the veins are generally superficial?
answer: An injury to the more important arteries would soon result in death, hence, they are deep-seated for protection. For the same reason the larger veins are deep-seated.

subject: Physiology
question: How and through what organ is the oxygen admitted to the blood?
answer: By a process called endosmosis the oxygen of the air passes through the tissues of the lungs.

subject: Physiology
question: State the functions of the lymphatics; of the lacteals.
answer: The lymphatics gather up materials that may still be of benefit to the blood. The lacteals carry nutrition from the intestines to the thoracic duct.

subject: Physiology
question: How can you distinguish in external hemorrhage, whether the blood comes from an artery or a vein?
answer: By observing the manner of the flow. The arteries throw out the blood by jets at each beat of the heart, while the veins bleed regularly.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the animal and vegetative functions of the body.
answer: The animal functions are sensation and volition. The vegetative functions, which are common to both animals and vegetables, are digestion, assimilation, absorption, secretion, excretion, growth, respiration, and generation.

subject: Physiology
question: What three evils result from tight lacing?
answer: 1st. It disturbs the circulation. 2nd. It restricts the action of the stomach and impairs digestion. 3rd. It prevents proper and sufficient respiration.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the respiratory and vocal organs.
answer: The respiratory and vocal organs are the larynx, the trachea, the bronchi, and the lungs.

subject: Physiology
question: State the immediate and ultimate object of respiration.
answer: The immediate object of respiration is the purification of the blood; the ultimate object is the production of heat, motion and nervous energy.

subject: Physiology
question: How does the amount of air inhaled compare with the quantity exhaled?
answer: The volume of air inhaled in an ordinary inspiration is about one pint, the quantity expelled, a little less than one pint.

subject: Physiology
question: In what way may the variety of tones of differ- ent voices be accounted for?
answer: The tones of different voices are modified by the shape and size of the vocal apparatus. A large larynx usually gives a deep-toned voice, a smaller one, a thin-toned or high-pitched voice.

subject: Physiology
question: Upon what does the strength of the voice de- pend?
answer: Upon the capacity of the chest, the development of the muscles used in vocalization, and the extent of vibration of the vocal cords.

subject: Physiology
question: Are the lungs filled and emptied completely by respiration? What is the importance of this provis- ion?
answer: No. If they were completely emptied the air cells would collapse, and the blood then in the lungs would not be purified.

subject: Physiology
question: What diseases are apt to attack the respiratory organs? How can these be provided against in the school room?
answer: Consumption, bronchitis, pneumonia, lung fever, asthma, etc. By having the room ventilated and warmed, and teaching the children to sit, stand, and walk erect, and breathe deeply and vigorously.

subject: Physiology
question: What anatomical changes cause the change in a boy's voice at puberty?
answer: The larynx grows larger, and the vocal cords longer and coarser, thus deepening the tones and lowering the pitch.

subject: Physiology
question: What three natural remedies for consumption are suggested by physiology and hygiene?
answer: Plenty of pure air properly breathed, sunlight, and frequent bodily exercise.

subject: Physiology
question: Explain the origin and progress of a cold.
answer: A cold frequently arises from a change in clothing, putting on a thinner garment, or sitting in a cool place, or a draft when heated. The skin becomes chilled and the perspiration checked. The pores are closed and the blood is driven to the lungs for purification. Oppression of the lungs ensues, breathing becomes difficult and the extra mucus is thrown off by coughing. From this condition fever, headache, pneumonia, or pleurisy is developed.

subject: Physiology
question: What is congestion? How indicated?
answer: Congestion is an excessive accumulation of blood in any part of the body. It is indicated by an unusual redness in the parts affected.

subject: Physiology
question: State the conditions upon which pure blood is dependent.
answer: Pure blood is obtained only by a healthy action of the respiratory organs, which in turn depends upon a constant and sufficient supply of pure air.

subject: Physiology
question: What physical evils result from impure blood?
answer: The carbonic acid is retained in the blood; the brain works sluggishly; the muscles become inactive; the heart acts imperfectly; the secretions are deteriorated; the food is not properly assimilated; and the whole body becomes weak.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the usual number of respirations per minute? How can the frequency be diminished?
answer: About eighteen per minute. By training and habit the number may be much diminished.

subject: Physiology
question: Into what two secondary systems may the ner- vous system be divided?
answer: The cerebro-spinal system, consisting of the brain, the spinal cord, and the nerves given off by them to all parts of the body, and the sympathetic system, composed of nerves and ganglia, mainly distributed to the viscera of the body.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) Describe the two kinds of matter which compose the nervous system. (b) State their func- tions.
answer: (a) The nervous system is composed of two kinds of matter, — the gray and the white. The gray consists of small, ashen-colored cells, forming a pulp-like substance; the white is composed of glistening, white fibers, averaging about 1/5000 of an inch in diameter. (b) The gray cells act as generators of nervous force, while the white fibers serve as conductors of this nervous force, having a velocity of about 100 feet per second.

subject: Physiology
question: Give a brief description of the human brain.
answer: The brain, the seat of the mind, is the great volume of nervous tissue lodged within the skull; it consists of two parts: — the cerebrum, or brain proper, and the cerebellum, or “little brain.” The cerebrum lies in the front and upper part of the skull, the cerebellum, in the lower and back part. The whole floats securely in a bed of liquid surrounded by three membranes, — the pia mater and the dura mater and the arachnoid.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the spinal cord? What means of protection suggest its importance?
answer: The spinal cord, or “ marrow,” is a cylindrical mass of soft nervous tissue, which occupies the tunnel fitted for it in the spinal column. It is composed of the same substances as the brain; but here the white matter surrounds the gray, instead of being encompassed by it. The importance of the spinal cord is apparent from the extreme care taken to protect it from external injury.

subject: Physiology
question: Where is the seat of pain? Illustrate.
answer: In the mind. If the “ funny bone ” behind the elbow receives a blow, the effect is at once sent to the brain, which refers the shock to the ends of the nerve in the third and fourth fingers, where the pain will seem to be.

subject: Physiology
question: Name in order the effects of alcohol on the nervous system.
answer: 1. Excitement. 2. Muscular weakness. 3. Mental weakness.

subject: Physiology
question: Why, in apoplexy, when the right side of the body becomes paralyzed, is the left side of the face usually affected?
answer: The nerves cross from the brain to the opposite side of the body, while the facial nerves come from the side of the brain affected.

subject: Physiology
question: Give examples of associated sensations in which nerves are excited by sight, hearing, and smell.
answer: Disagreeable odors and unpleasant sights produce nausea; the savory odor of cooking food and the peeling of a lemon make the mouth water; while the rasping sound heard in filing a saw produces in some persons the utmost irritability.

subject: Physiology
question: What are the functions of the cerebrum and the cerebellum respectively?
answer: The cerebrum is the instrument through which the powers of memory, reason, and judgment manifest themselves. The cerebellum presides over the muscular movements of the body.

subject: Physiology
question: Name the nerves of special sense and state in what they differ from other nerves.
answer: The nerves of special sense are the auditory, optic, and olfactory nerves. They differ from other nerves in being neither sensitive nor motor, their office being simply to convey the sense of hearing, seeing, and smelling.

subject: Physiology
question: Name habits which particularly impair the nervous system.
answer: The opium habit, the use of alcohol, tobacco, and the excessive use of tea and coffee.

subject: Physiology
question: What conditions are essential to the highest mental development and vigor?
answer: Each faculty of mind should receive its due share of cultivation and exercise.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) What is meant by "reflex action of the spinal cord?" (b) Give examples. (c) State its use.
answer: (a) The reflex action of the spinal cord is the involuntary action of certain muscles independent of the agency of the will. An impression made on the surface of the skin, is conveyed to the cord, which reflects back the motor impulse to the muscles so as to excite them to action. (b) Examples are numerous: nearly every act of our daily routine is an illustration—walking, eating, standing erect. (c) By the reflex action of the cord we are protected from a thousand perils. We involuntarily throw up our hands as a shield from danger, we instinctively wink to protect the eye, and upon seizing a heated substance, we instantly drop it before the command could come from the brain.

subject: Physiology
question: Describe the globe of the eye and name its parts.
answer: The globe of the eye consists of three concentric layers—the sclerotica, with the cornea in front; the choroidea, with the iris in front; and the retina, which is internal. These compose most of the solid part of the eyeball, which is a hollow sphere filled with three semi-fluid substances—the aqueous humor, the crystalline lens, and the vitreous humor.

subject: Physiology
question: How do we change the vision in looking from near objects to distant ones in the same direction?
answer: The convexity of the lens is changed by means of ciliary muscles. The lens is made more convex for near objects and less convex for those more distant.

subject: Physiology
question: (a) What four classes of substances excite the sense of taste? (b) Locate the sense of taste.
answer: (a) The sour, as certain acids; the sweet, as sirup and sugar; the bitter, as quinine; and the salt, as common salt. (b) The margin and the tip of the tongue.

subject: Physiology
question: Where is the sense of touch most delicate? How may this be shown?
answer: The sense of touch is most acute on the palmar surface of the fingers. If two needles one-twentieth of an inch apart are pressed against the end of the finger two impressions are felt. No where else on the body will this occur when the needles are so near together.

subject: Physiology
question: What purpose does the skin serve?
answer: The skin aids in keeping the tissues and organs in their places, and protects the delicate nerves, lymphatics and blood vessels found beneath it.

subject: Physiology
question: State the office of perspiration.
answer: It regulates the temperature of the body. When exercise warms the body the perspiration is exuded upon the skin and its evaporation reduces the temperature and keeps the system from being overheated.

subject: Zoology
question: Explain the terms biology, zoölogy, and natural history.
answer: Biology (bios, life; and logos, discourse) is the study of living objects of all kinds. It includes the sciences of Botany and Zoology. Zoology (zoön, animal; and logos, discourse) treats of animals. The term Natural History, formerly applied to the study of all natural objects, is now limited to Zoology.

subject: Zoology
question: How does the nutrition of plants differ from that of animals?
answer: Plants live upon purely dead or inorganic substances, as water, carbonic acid, ammonia, converting these into organic substances; as starch, cellulose, sugar, etc. Animals have no power of living on inorganic substances; they require ready-made organic compounds, which plants furnish.

subject: Zoology
question: Give a comprehensive definition of animals
answer: Animals are living beings, nourished wholly by organic food, and which have sensation and the power of voluntary motion, consuming oxygen and giving off carbonic acid.

subject: Zoology
question: Name and define the three great physiological functions.
answer: 1. Functions of Nutrition, those by which an animal is able to live, grow and maintain its existence as an individual. 2. Functions of Reproduction, those by which the perpetuation of the species is insured. 3. Functions of Relation, those by means of which external objects are brought into relation with the organism, and by which it in turn reacts upon the outer world.

subject: Zoology
question: Name some of the most celebrated naturalists who have devoted much attention to zoölogy.
answer: Aristotle, Linneas, Cuvier, Buffon, and Agassiz.

subject: Zoology
question: Show in what manner we are dependent upon the animal creation.
answer: First, a great portion of our food is derived from the animals of the forest, field and waters. Second, all our most valuable articles of apparel we get from this source. Third, dyes, varnishes, glues, ivory, bone, and a thousand other articles employed in the arts we take from the animal world.

subject: Zoology
question: How is mankind benefited by a knowledge of the habits of animals?
answer: Zoology teaches mankind what animals are useful to him, and what ones are harmful. It teaches us how to protect those which may be of use, and how to destroy those which do us harm.

subject: Zoology
question: (a) What are fossils? (b) In what way do they aid the geologist?
answer: (a) The remains of ancient organic bodies discovered in the earth. (b) Fossils aid the geologist in tracing out the different rock formations, and finding the coal and other materials essential to supply the wants of civilized man.

subject: Zoology
question: How is paleontology dependent upon zoölogy?
answer: Fossil remains of animals can be understood only when studied by the aid of facts and principles of zoölogy.

subject: Zoology
question: Name the systems of organs found in those animals exhibiting the highest order of structure.
answer: Respiratory, digestive, absorbent, circulatory, secretory, excretory, motory, reproductive and nervous systems.

subject: Zoology
question: Define the terms absorption and exhalation.
answer: Absorption is the act by which organisms imbibe into their tissues the fluids which surround them. Exhalation is the act of sending forth fluids in the form of vapor through the external and internal surfaces of the animal.

subject: Zoology
question: State what is meant by a cell in animal structure.
answer: A cell is the ultimate structural element of the animal composition. It is a minute vesicle filled with a viscid liquid named protoplasm; i.e., the first to form. Within the vesicle there is a central particle termed the nucleus, and within this, there is, in many cases, a still smaller particle called nucleolus.

subject: Zoology
question: Upon what is the study of zoölogy chiefly based?
answer: Upon the classification of animals according to their relations and affinities.

subject: Zoology
question: State the general plan of classification of the animal kingdom by naming the groups beginning with the highest division.
answer: The first division is into Subkingdoms or Branches; 2nd. Classes; 3rd. Orders; 4th. Families; 5th. Genera; 6th. Species.

subject: Zoology
question: According to modern naturalists, what principles determine the classification of animals into classes, orders, families, etc.?
answer: Subkingdoms are characterized by plan of structure; classes, by the manner in which the plan is executed; orders, by the complication of structure; families, by form, as determined by structure; genera, by details of execution in special parts; species, by the relation of individuals to one another, and by the proportion of their parts.

subject: Zoology
question: Upon what principle is all scientific classification of animals based?
answer: Upon structure and upon form as determined by structure.

subject: Zoology
question: Name the six subkingdoms into which the animal kingdom is commonly divided.
answer: 1. Vertebrata. 2. Articulata. 3. Mollusca. 4. Echinodermata. 5. Coelenterata. 6. Protozoa.

subject: Zoology
question: What animals constitute the Vertebrates?
answer: All animals which have an internal jointed skeleton, and a brain and spinal cord along the dorsal side.

subject: Zoology
question: Name the five classes composing the Vertebrata.
answer: Mammalia, Aves, Reptilia, Amphibia, Pisces.

subject: Zoology
question: How do the bones of vertebrates differ from shells of mollusks?
answer: The bones are living and vascular animal tissues, growing and changing by internal additions and modifications. Shells grow only by additions to and modifications of the circumference.

subject: Zoology
question: In what respect do the teeth differ from bone?
answer: The teeth are in part composed of bony material called cement, but their principal substances are dentine and enamel; enamel being harder than any other tissue of the body, having but a trace of animal tissue, the teeth are far more durable than the bones.

subject: Zoology
question: What are the characteristics of the Mammalia?
answer: The Mammalia are warm-blooded, air-breathing vertebrates, which bring forth living young, and nourish them with milk. Their skin is usually covered with hair, fur or wool. Their lungs are separated by a diaphragm from the abdominal cavity. The heart has four cavities; the circulation of the blood is double and complete.

subject: Zoology
question: Name the orders of the Mammalia, and after each give an example.
answer: Bimana, man; Quadrumana, monkey; Carnivora, lion; Ungulata, horse; Proboscidea, elephant; Sirenia, dugong; Hyracoidea, daman; Cetacea, whale; Chiroptera, bat; Insectivora, mole; Rodentia, beaver; Edentata, anteater; Marsupialia, opossum; Monotremata, duckbill.

subject: Zoology
question: In what consists the superiority of the frame of man over that of other animals?
answer: The framework of man is superior to that of the animals in its adaptability for the greatest variety of movements.

subject: Zoology
question: Why are monkeys sometimes classed as Pedimana?
answer: Because of their power of opposing the great toe to the other toes, making the hind feet become hands.

subject: Zoology
question: State the anatomical distinctions between man and the monkey.
answer: Monkeys may be distinguished from man anatomically by the spinal opening in the cranium being in the posterior third of the base; by the single curve of the vertebral column; by the shortness of the thumb which does not reach the base of the index finger; by the long and narrow pelvis; by the obtuse angle of the foot with the leg; and by the disproportionate length of the arms, which in most species, reach to the knee.

subject: Zoology
question: Explain the meaning of complete and double circulation as applied to mammals.
answer: The circulation in mammals is complete because all the blood circulates through the lungs before going through the body; and it is said to be double because the blood passes through two sets of capillary vessels, one set belonging to the lungs, the other set to the body.

subject: Zoology
question: How can you prove that cartilage is the basis of all the bones?
answer: This may be shown by placing a bone in weak muriatic acid for a few days, when the mineral substance will be dissolved, and the cartilage having the same form and size as the bone will remain.

subject: Zoology
question: Write the dental formula of man.
answer: Incisors, 2-2/2-2; Canine, 1-1/1-1; Pre-molars, 2-2/2-2 Molars, 3-3/3-3 = 32.

subject: Zoology
question: What is meant by "facial angle?" How does man compare in facial angle with some of the lower orders?
answer: The "facial angle" is formed by the intersection of two lines projected in the following manner: the first extends from the lower opening of the ear to the base of the nose; the second starting from the most prominent part of the forehead, intersects the first at the most prominent part of the upper jaw. In the White race this angle varies from 75° to 95°; in the ape it is 40°; in the dog, 20°.

subject: Zoology
question: Name the three families of the Quadrumana, and state which most resembles man.
answer: The three families of the quadrumana are the Simiidæ, the Cebidæ, and the Lemuridæ. The Simiidæ resembles man most.

subject: Zoology
question: Can the Quadrumana be trained to be of any service to man? Give your reasons.
answer: None have ever been trained to render any useful service, and such training is probably impossible from their disposition, being selfish, crafty, thievish, and malicious.

subject: Zoology
question: State to what families the following monkeys belong: Mandrill, Spider Monkey, Aye Aye, Gorilla, Chimpanzee.
answer: The Mandrill, to the Simiidæ; the Spider Monkey, to the Cebidæ; the Aye Aye, to the Lemuridæ; the Gorilla and the Chimpanzee, to the Simiidæ.

subject: Zoology
question: What are the prominent characteristics of the Carnivora?
answer: 1st. They feed wholly or mainly on flesh. 2nd. They have long, sharp teeth, fitted for cutting and tearing rather than grinding. 3rd. The stomach is simple and the intestines relatively short, perfectly adapted to their easily digestible food. 4th. Their feet are provided with toes, which are often armed with sharp claws. 5th. In most cases the Carnivora are without clavicles.

subject: Zoology
question: Into what families are the Carnivora divided?
answer: Felidæ, as the lion, cat; Viverridæ, civet, ich-neumon; Hyenidæ, hyena; Canidæ, dog, wolf; Mustelidæ, weasel, otter; Ursidæ, bear; Procyonidæ, raccoon; Otariidæ, eared seal, sea bear; Phocidæ, common seal; Rosmaridæ, walrus.

subject: Zoology
